title: Extending an Image Beyond Its Borders
concept: Outpainting generates plausible new content beyond an image's original edges, effectively expanding the canvas while staying visually consistent.

exercises.prompt: You take a photo of a mountain, but the top of the peak got cut off by the edge of the picture. What would you want an AI tool to do?

options.text: Add more mountain and sky beyond the edge so the photo looks complete
Delete the mountain from the photo entirely
Turn the whole photo black and white

exercises.explain: Exactly! You'd want the AI to imagine and add more of the scene past the original edge — that's called outpainting.

cards.title: What's Beyond the Frame?
cards.body: Ever looked at a photo and wished you could see just a little more — what's past the left edge, or above the top? AI can actually imagine that for you and paint it in, stretching your picture bigger than it ever was!

cards.title: Outpainting
cards.body: Outpainting is when AI generates brand-new image content that extends beyond the original picture's borders. It looks at the edges of your photo and creates matching new areas, making the canvas bigger while keeping everything looking like it belongs together.

cards.title: Like Finishing a Puzzle With No Picture
cards.body: Imagine a jigsaw puzzle where the middle is done, but the edges are empty with no box picture to guide you. A master puzzle artist looks at the colors and patterns near the edge and paints new puzzle pieces that blend in perfectly, growing the puzzle bigger. That's outpainting — extending what already exists in a way that fits.

cards.title: Widening a Landscape
cards.body: Say you have a narrow photo of a forest that only shows a few trees. Using outpainting, an AI can add more trees, sky, and ground on both sides, turning your small photo into a wide, sweeping forest scene — as if the camera had captured a much bigger view all along.

cards.title: Myth: It's Just Stretching the Photo
cards.body: Stretching a photo just pulls and blurs existing pixels — it doesn't add real new detail. Outpainting is different: the AI actually creates brand-new, detailed content (new trees, clouds, buildings) that never existed before, matching the style and lighting of the original.

cards.title: Original vs. Outpainted
cards.body: Compare a tightly cropped photo to its outpainted version — same subject, same style, but a much bigger world around it.

left.text: A small, cropped image showing only a dog sitting in a tiny patch of grass

right.text: The same dog, but now surrounded by a full park scene with trees, a path, and sky that AI invented

exercises.prompt: What does outpainting mainly do to an image?

options.text: Generates new content beyond the image's original edges
Converts the photo into a sketch

exercises.explain: Outpainting extends the canvas by creating new, matching content past the original borders of the picture.

exercises.prompt: Outpainting simply stretches the existing pixels of a photo to make it bigger.
exercises.explain: False — outpainting creates entirely new, detailed content that fits the style of the original, rather than just stretching existing pixels.

exercises.sentence: Outpainting expands an image by generating new content beyond its original ___.
exercises.explain: Outpainting extends the picture past its original borders, adding new, believable content around the edges.

exercises.prompt: A designer has a small square photo of a castle and wants a wide banner image showing the castle with a much bigger sky and landscape around it, matching the original style. What should they use?

options.text: Outpainting, to generate new matching scenery beyond the original edges
Simply zooming in on the castle

exercises.explain: Outpainting is exactly built for this — expanding a small image into a wider scene by inventing consistent new content around it.

exercises.prompt: Boss Challenge: You have a tall portrait photo of a person, but you need a wide horizontal image for a website banner, with extra scenery visible on both the left and right sides. Which technique should you use?

options.text: Outpainting — to generate new, matching scenery on both sides of the photo
Cropping the photo tighter around the person
Turning the photo into black and white

exercises.explain: Outpainting is the right tool because it can generate brand-new, believable content extending past the left and right edges, turning a tall photo into a wide banner while keeping everything visually consistent.
